html:
[605]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 精神衰甚 - Literally 'spirit very weakened,' used to describe a state of extreme physical or mental fatigue.
  • 饮食少思 - Literally 'less appetite for food,' indicating a lack of desire to eat.
  • 若大病初愈 - Literally 'as if just recovered from a serious illness,' suggesting a state of lingering weakness.
  • 每食因饿梦难堪 - Literally 'every time eating due to hunger dreams become unbearable,' describing the distress caused by hunger-related dreams.
  • 知乞人之苦 - Literally 'knowing the suffering of beggars,' indicating empathy for the plight of beggars.
  • 凡有乞丐到门，必令家人多少速与之，使彼转乞 - Literally 'whenever beggars come to the door, surely command family members to give them something, so they can turn around and beg elsewhere,' describing a charitable act.
  • 一忆梦凶，不胜觳觫 - Literally 'once remembering the frightening dream, unable to bear the trembling,' describing the fear and anxiety caused by recalling a distressing dream.
  • 恐梦情复恶，因思祷神祗以解除之 - Literally 'fearing the dream's condition will become worse, thus thinking of praying to deities to relieve it,' indicating a desire to seek divine intervention.
  • 附近庵观，致祭殆遍 - Literally 'nearby temples and shrines, almost all have been worshipped,' describing the extensive efforts to pray at various religious sites.
  • 至十日，所梦如故，惟饿梦不再 - Literally 'by the tenth day, the dreams remain as before, only the hunger dreams no longer occur,' indicating that some dreams persist while others have ceased.
  • 某自计连梦已及十日，再受一夜之痛，亦可少休，犹不幸中之幸 - Literally 'I calculated that my dreams have lasted for ten days, another night's pain is also a little rest, still a kind of misfortune,' describing a sense of relief amidst ongoing distress.
  • 及夜乡晨，有人执铁钩将拔其舌 - Literally 'by the time of dawn, someone holding an iron hook is about to pull out my tongue,' describing a terrifying and painful experience.
  • 此前此未有之事。吾又作何孽，而复以此加之？ - Literally 'This is an unprecedented event. What sin did I commit, and why is this being added to me?' expressing confusion and self-doubt.
  • 非毁神圣之所致 - Literally 'not caused by destroying the sacred,' indicating that the punishment is not due to disrespecting deities.
  • 忍痛受拔而不出声 - Literally 'enduring the pain and being pulled without making a sound,' describing stoic suffering.
  • 及醒，自咬其舌，鲜血满口 - Literally 'upon waking, biting my own tongue, blood fills my mouth,' describing a self-inflicted wound.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 乞人 - Refers to beggars, a common social issue in ancient China.
  • 庵观 - Refers to temples and shrines, important religious sites in Chinese culture.
  • 神祗 - Refers to deities and spirits, central to Chinese religious beliefs.
  • 土木偶 - Refers to wooden and earthen idols, often used in religious rituals.
  • 因果 - Refers to the concept of cause and effect, a fundamental belief in Buddhism and other religions.
  • 孤贫栖流所 - Refers to shelters for orphans and the poor, reflecting social welfare concerns.

[606]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 某以饿梦之不复，拔舌之更添 - Personal statement indicating recurring nightmares and additional punishments, likely due to past sins or mistakes.
  • 知祸福皆由自求 - Philosophical statement suggesting that one's fortune or misfortune is determined by one's own actions.
  • 反素行，改新过 - Indicates a change in behavior, turning away from previous actions and correcting new mistakes.
  • 拔舌之责，不期免而免 - Refers to the speaker's unexpected release from the punishment of having their tongue pulled out, symbolizing a form of karmic retribution.
  • 外财不贪，铜汁不入于口 - A vow to avoid greed and material temptations, specifically avoiding the consumption of copper, which may be symbolic of wealth or luxury.
  • 荤腥不茹，沸汤不及于身 - A commitment to a vegetarian diet and avoiding excessive heat, possibly symbolizing the avoidance of harmful or impure substances.
  • 收屠人之本资，身不化为豕 - A vow to stop engaging in butchery, with the belief that this will prevent the speaker from being reborn as a pig.
  • 绝私交之妇女，妻不梦为娼 - A commitment to avoiding inappropriate relationships, with the hope that this will prevent the speaker's wife from dreaming of becoming a prostitute.
  • 诸事斟酌，不敢妄为 - A general statement of caution and careful consideration in all actions.
  • 十夜之苦，月馀得去其七 - Refers to a period of suffering lasting ten nights, during which the speaker experienced relief after several months.
  • 人言孝能感动鬼神，逢凶化吉 - A common belief that filial piety can move spirits and turn bad luck into good fortune.
  • 自思父母双亡，孝无由进 - A reflection on the speaker's inability to practice filial piety due to the death of both parents.
  • 结茅屋于父母墓侧 - A symbolic act of building a simple hut near the parents' graves, showing respect and remembrance.
  • 谨具庶馐楮帛，竭诚祭扫 - A detailed description of the speaker's sincere and elaborate rituals for honoring the deceased.
  • 儿不孝，不能竭力事生，悔之无及 - A heartfelt expression of regret for not being able to serve the parents while they were alive.
  • 愿从于地下，少进定省，以报大德 - A wish to follow the parents into the afterlife to repay their great kindness.
  • 伏地不起 - A gesture of deep sorrow and submission.
  • 晚宿茅屋中，每日晨、午、暮三次致祭 - A description of the speaker's daily routine of visiting the grave and performing rituals.
  • 两越月，果无恶梦 - After two months of consistent ritual practice, the speaker no longer experiences bad dreams.
  • 里人喜其改过，劝归之 - The local community approves of the speaker's repentance and encourages them to return home.
  • 梦身居冰中，十日一次 - A recurring nightmare where the speaker is trapped in ice, occurring once every ten days.
  • 忽忆为尼之女及笄，赎回，择婿嫁之 - A sudden memory of a young woman who was a nun and had reached the age of marriage, leading the speaker to redeem her and arrange her marriage.
  • 寒冰之狱亦绝 - The speaker's recurring nightmare of being in a cold ice prison finally ceases.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 饿梦 - Refers to nightmares or bad dreams, often associated with spiritual or moral guilt.
  • 拔舌 - A form of punishment in hell, where the tongue is pulled out as a consequence of lying or speaking ill.
  • 孝 - Filial piety, a core virtue in Confucian ethics, emphasizing respect and care for one's parents.
  • 庶馐 - Refers to various offerings, often including food and other items, used in rituals.
  • 楮帛 - Paper money and silk used as offerings in funerary rituals.
  • 定省 - The act of visiting parents in the morning and evening, a traditional practice of filial piety.
  • 及笄 - The age at which a girl becomes a woman, typically around 15 years old, marking her eligibility for marriage.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence structure is compact, but the meaning is clear: the speaker is experiencing recurring nightmares and additional punishments.
  • The cause-and-effect relationship is clear, but the sentence is elliptical, requiring expansion for clarity.
  • The sentence is concise, but the timeline and the nature of the suffering are important to convey.
  • The sentence is a common belief, but the connection between filial piety and supernatural intervention is significant.
  • The sentence is a personal reflection, but the emotional weight of the speaker's regret is crucial.
  • The sentence is a detailed description of the speaker's actions, but the symbolic significance of each element is important.
  • The sentence is a heartfelt expression of regret, but the emotional impact is significant.
  • The sentence is a wish for the afterlife, but the speaker's desire to repay the parents' kindness is central.
  • The gesture is simple, but the emotional context is important.
  • The sentence describes a routine, but the consistency and sincerity of the rituals are key.
  • The sentence is a conclusion, but the relief and transformation are significant.
  • The sentence is a community reaction, but the approval and encouragement are important.
  • The sentence is a recurring nightmare, but the frequency and nature of the dream are significant.
  • The sentence is a memory and action, but the suddenness and the outcome are important.
  • The sentence is a final resolution, but the cessation of the nightmare is significant.

[607]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 虚白道人 - Self-referential phrase used by the author to introduce his own commentary.
  • 至人无梦 - Daoist concept meaning 'the perfected person has no dreams,' suggesting a state of enlightenment or detachment from worldly desires.
  • 旦昼之所为寓恶意 - Means 'the actions taken during the day harbor ill intent,' implying that one's daytime behavior reflects negative thoughts.
  • 意念之所存反中行 - Means 'the thoughts held in mind run counter to the proper path,' suggesting a deviation from moral or ethical conduct.
  • 神人假之以示儆 - Means 'the divine uses it to warn,' indicating that the dream serves as a warning from a higher power.
  • 假以罪之，使暗自忍受也 - Means 'it is also used to punish, making one endure it silently,' suggesting that the dream is a form of divine retribution.
  • 欲禳恶梦，诸侯修德，大夫修官，士修身 - Means 'to ward off evil dreams, princes should cultivate virtue, officials should perform their duties, and scholars should cultivate themselves,' offering a prescription for avoiding bad dreams through self-improvement.
  • 庶恶人稍知自戢 - Means 'so that evildoers may gradually learn to restrain themselves,' suggesting that the dream serves as a deterrent to wrongdoing.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 虚白道人 - A self-referential title used by the author, likely indicating his role as a Daoist or philosophical commentator.
  • 至人 - A Daoist term for a perfected or enlightened individual.
  • 神人 - A divine or supernatural being in Daoist or Chinese cosmology.
  • 杨子厚 - A proper name, possibly referring to a historical figure or the author of the text.
  • 趾离 - A cryptic reference, possibly alluding to a specific figure or concept in Chinese mythology or philosophy.

Proper Nouns

  • 虚白道人 - The self-referential title of the author.
  • 杨子厚 - A proper name, possibly referring to a historical figure or the author of the text.
  • 趾离 - A cryptic reference, possibly alluding to a specific figure or concept in Chinese mythology or philosophy.
  • 上元李瑜 - The name of the annotator of the text.

[608]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 無惧 - Means 'fearless' or 'without fear.'
  • 勒索 - Means 'to extort' or 'to demand by force.'
  • 正人 - Means 'upright person' or 'virtuous person.'
  • 事属不测 - Means 'the matter is unpredictable.'
  • 受耻辱于鬼狐 - Means 'to suffer humiliation from ghosts and fox spirits.'
  • 漠然置之 - Means 'to treat with indifference' or 'to disregard.'
  • 妻死，家无系怜 - Means 'his wife died, and he had no one to rely on.'
  • 恃艺糊口于四方 - Means 'relying on his skills to make a living in various places.'
  • 游至平阳 - Means 'traveling to Pingyang.'
  • 为狐祟者 - Means 'someone being plagued by a fox spirit.'
  • 设坛焚符 - Means 'setting up an altar and burning talismans.'
  • 不应 - Means 'did not respond' or 'did not work.'
  • 无可如何 - Means 'helpless' or 'unable to do anything.'
  • 丽人 - Means 'beautiful woman.'
  • 吾之来，非君符水有灵，实吾自至 - Means 'My coming is not because of the power of your talismans, but because I came on my own.'
  • 属至戚 - Means 'close relatives.'
  • 奉亲命来相邀 - Means 'coming at the command of my parents to invite you.'
  • 胞妹 - Means 'younger sister.'
  • 幼字于君 - Means 'engaged to you when she was young.'
  • 负约别娶 - Means 'broke the engagement and married someone else.'
  • 欲为妹另择配 - Means 'wanted to find another match for my sister.'
  • 妹不可 - Means 'my sister refused.'
  • 迄今犹守贞以俟 - Means 'she has remained chaste until now, waiting for you.'
  • 邀君辱临 - Means 'inviting you to come.'
  • 就婚耳 - Means 'to get married.'
  • 何急也 - Means 'why the hurry?'
  • 吾去，君所医者之病即愈 - Means 'When I leave, the patient you treated will recover.'
  • 受其谢 - Means 'accept their gratitude.'
  • 五日后，奉迓台驾于东门外，午时为度 - Means 'Five days later, I will meet you at the east gate at noon.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 山西 - Refers to Shanxi province.
  • 符录 - A traditional practice involving talismans and incantations.
  • 地理阳宅 - Refers to the practice of feng shui and geomancy.
  • 占卦算命 - Traditional Chinese divination practices.
  • 鬼狐 - Refers to spirits and fox spirits in Chinese folklore.
  • 平阳 - A city in Shanxi province.

Proper Nouns

  • 刘希文 - The name of the male protagonist.
  • 许翠娥 - The name of the female character.
  • 山西 - The location where Liu is from.
  • 平阳 - The location where the story takes place.

[609]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 初 - Indicates the beginning or start of a story, often used to introduce a narrative.
  • 拾一小狐 - Literally 'picks up a small fox,' used to describe an action of taking something.
  • 似初产 - Seems newly born, describing the appearance of the fox.
  • 喂以米粥 - Feeds it with rice porridge, describing the action of feeding.
  • 三日眼睁能走 - After three days, it opens its eyes and can walk, describing the growth and development of the fox.
  • 两越月如小犬 - After two months, it looks like a small dog, describing the appearance of the fox after a period of time.
  • 其毛如濡可爱 - Its fur is soft and lovely, describing the texture and appearance of the fox's fur.
  • 常戏弄之 - Often plays with it, describing Liu's interaction with the fox.
  • 当昼亦有拥之而卧之时 - Sometimes holds it while sleeping during the day, describing Liu's behavior with the fox.
  • 父见之，戏曰 - Father sees it and jokes, describing the father's reaction.
  • 此汝妇 - This is your wife, a joke made by the father.
  • 又三年 - Another three years, describing the passage of time.
  • 如巨犬 - Looks like a large dog, describing the size of the fox.
  • 刘每食必食之 - Liu always eats with it, describing Liu's habit of eating with the fox.
  • 忽不见 - Suddenly disappears, describing the sudden disappearance of the fox.
  • 刘思之而泣 - Liu thinks about it and cries, describing Liu's emotional response.
  • 父戏之曰 - Father jokes with him, describing the father's response.
  • 此事刘固识之 - Liu definitely remembers this, emphasizing Liu's memory.
  • 而意料不及此 - But he did not expect this, describing Liu's surprise.
  • 及刘受病家之谢 - When Liu receives thanks from the sick family, describing a specific event.
  • 已及五日 - After five days, describing the duration of time.
  • 遂如女邀 - Then he goes to meet a woman, describing Liu's action.
  • 出其东门 - Leaves through the east gate, describing the location.
  • 见一小车驾驹俟周道 - Sees a small carriage with a horse waiting on the road, describing what Liu sees.
  • 御者见刘曰 - The driver sees Liu and says, describing the driver's introduction.
  • 君刘贵人耶 - Are you Mr. Liu, the nobleman? a question from the driver.
  • 吾非贵人，吾刘希文也 - I am not a nobleman, I am Liu Xiwén, Liu's response.
  • 吾即迎接贵人者 - I am the one who comes to welcome the nobleman, the driver's response.
  • 即车中取衣冠奉刘 - Immediately takes clothes and a hat from the carriage and gives them to Liu, describing the driver's action.
  • 刘衣毕升车 - Liu dresses and gets into the carriage, describing Liu's action.
  • 展铃而发 - Rings the bell and starts the journey, describing the start of the journey.
  • 不多时，数里外忽睹城垣 - Not long after, he suddenly sees city walls a few miles away, describing what Liu sees.
  • 此何处 - Where is this place? a question from Liu.
  • 此岳阳城也 - This is the city of Yueyang, the driver's response.
  • 一百二十里路，倏忽即到耶 - One hundred and twenty miles, and it seems to have arrived instantly? a rhetorical question from Liu.
  • 未几，至一庄首 - Soon, they arrive at the entrance of a village, describing their arrival.
  • 见数人盛服并列，若候客 - Sees several people dressed in fine clothes standing in a row, as if waiting for a guest, describing what Liu sees.
  • 彼悉奉迓贵人者 - They are all here to welcome the nobleman, the driver's response.
  • 刘闻之，下，整衣与揖 - Liu hears this, gets off the carriage, straightens his clothes, and bows, describing Liu's action.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 刘希文 - A character in the story, likely a historical or literary figure.
  • 岳阳城 - Yueyang City, a real place in China known for its historical and cultural significance.
  • 小车驾驹 - A small carriage with a horse, a common mode of transportation in ancient times.
  • 衣冠 - Clothes and a hat, symbols of social status and respect.
  • 御者 - The driver of the carriage, often a servant or attendant.
  • 病家之谢 - Thanks from a sick family, indicating a specific event in the story.

Proper Nouns

  • 刘希文 - A character in the story.
  • 岳阳城 - Yueyang City, a real place in China.
  • 小车驾驹 - A small carriage with a horse.
  • 衣冠 - Clothes and a hat.
  • 御者 - The driver of the carriage.
  • 病家之谢 - Thanks from a sick family.

[610]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 共陪刘至一大门 - To accompany Liu to the grand gate together, indicating a formal or ceremonial gathering.
  • 悬灯结彩 - Literally 'hang lights and tie up decorations,' used to describe festive preparations.
  • 花烛之仪 - Refers to the traditional wedding ceremony with red candles, symbolizing the union of two families.
  • 宛如世家 - Means 'as if from a noble family,' emphasizing the grandeur and formality of the event.
  • 不胜之喜 - Means 'overwhelming joy,' expressing intense happiness.
  • 卿之族徒，仆已悉 - Means 'I have learned about your family members,' where '族徒' refers to family members or relatives.
  • 忘诸乎 - A rhetorical question meaning 'have you forgotten?'
  • 豁然 - Means 'suddenly enlightened' or 'became clear.'
  • 行矣 - Means 'go' or 'depart.'
  • 属不情 - Means 'it would be unreasonable' or 'it would be unfeeling.'
  • 诸日奔波 - Means 'endless wandering' or 'constant travel.'
  • 大有违碍 - Means 'greatly obstructive' or 'highly inconvenient.'
  • 未一归耶 - Means 'not once returned?'
  • 勿庸 - Means 'no need' or 'not necessary.'
  • 自能寻之 - Means 'I can find you myself.'
  • 罔功 - Means 'fruitless' or 'unsuccessful.'
  • 诺 - Means 'yes' or 'agreed.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 刘 - The name 'Liu' is a common Chinese surname, but without additional context, it cannot be identified as a specific historical figure.
  • 翠娥 - The name 'Cui E' is a character in the story, and her role is that of a woman with supernatural abilities.
  • 岳家 - Refers to the wife's family, often used in the context of a daughter-in-law visiting her parents.
  • 仆 - A humble self-reference, used by Liu to show respect.
  • 卿 - A term of endearment or respect, used by Liu to address Cui E.
  • 投宿 - Means 'to stay overnight' or 'to lodge.'
  • 预言 - Means 'to predict' or 'to foretell.'
  • 未一归耶 - A rhetorical question asking if someone has not returned even once.
  • 勿庸 - A polite way of saying 'not necessary' or 'no need.'
  • 自能寻之 - Indicates that someone can find their way without assistance.
  • 罔功 - Means 'fruitless' or 'unsuccessful.'
  • 诺 - A simple affirmative response, often used in formal or polite contexts.

Proper Nouns

  • 刘 - The name of the male character.
  • 翠娥 - The name of the female character with supernatural abilities.
  • 岳家 - The family of the wife, often used in the context of a daughter-in-law visiting her parents.
  • 仆 - A humble self-reference, used by Liu to show respect.
  • 卿 - A term of endearment or respect, used by Liu to address Cui E.
  • 投宿 - Means 'to stay overnight' or 'to lodge.'
  • 预言 - Means 'to predict' or 'to foretell.'
  • 未一归耶 - A rhetorical question asking if someone has not returned even once.
  • 勿庸 - A polite way of saying 'not necessary' or 'no need.'
  • 自能寻之 - Indicates that someone can find their way without assistance.
  • 罔功 - Means 'fruitless' or 'unsuccessful.'
  • 诺 - A simple affirmative response, often used in formal or polite contexts.

[611]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 刘游至一山庄 - 刘游 refers to Liu You, a character in the story. 至一山庄 means arrived at a mountain village.
  • 闻号泣者凡四、五家 - 闻号泣者 means heard crying, and 凡四、五家 means about four or five households.
  • 何丧之同也 - A rhetorical question meaning Why are the deaths the same?
  • 东南山有蛇精二 - 东南山 means eastern south mountain, and 蛇精二 means two snake spirits.
  • 变化人形，雌惑壮男，雄祟丽妇 - 变化人形 means transform into human form, 雌惑壮男 means the female deceives strong men, and 雄祟丽妇 means the male torments beautiful women.
  • 且时吞噬冲幼子女 - 且时 means and at times, 吞噬 means devour, and 冲幼子女 means young children.
  • 悉丧子、丧妇与丧幼年男女者也 - 悉 means all, 丧子 means lost their sons, 丧妇 means lost their wives, and 丧幼年男女者也 means lost their young boys and girls.
  • 何以知为蛇精 - 何以 means how can you know, and 知为蛇精 means that they are snake spirits.
  • 庄首有巨湾，水极澄澈 - 庄首 means the head of the village, 巨湾 means a large bay, and 水极澄澈 means the water is extremely clear.
  • 是物每夜同来饮水 - 是物 means these creatures, 每夜 means every night, and 同来饮水 means come together to drink water.
  • 可得睹乎 - 可得 means can be seen, and 睹 means see.
  • 二更后，遥见明灯四盏 - 二更后 means after the second watch, and 遥见明灯四盏 means saw four bright lamps from afar.
  • 明灯即蛇眼 - 明灯 means bright lamps, and 即蛇眼 means are the snake eyes.
  • 长约五丈，粗可一围 - 长约五丈 means about five zhang long, and 粗可一围 means about one chi thick.
  • 吾能除此毒害 - 吾能 means I can, and 除此毒害 means eliminate this poison.
  • 急市尖刀数十把，预备火炮响器 - 急市 means urgently buy, 尖刀 means sharp knives, 数十把 means dozens of, and 预备火炮响器 means prepare firecrackers and noisemakers.
  • 刘同庄人于蛇去路草莽中埋尖刀，微露其锋，半步一把 - 刘同庄人 means Liu and the villagers, 蛇去路 means the path the snakes take, 草莽中 means in the grass, 埋尖刀 means bury sharp knives, 微露其锋 means slightly expose their blades, and 半步一把 means one every half step.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 刘游 - A fictional character in the story.
  • 蛇精 - Snake spirits, often associated with folklore and mythology in Chinese culture.
  • 明灯 - Bright lamps, often used in folklore to represent eyes or other features.
  • 尖刀 - Sharp knives, used for hunting or defense.
  • 火炮响器 - Firecrackers and noisemakers, used to scare away evil spirits.

Proper Nouns

  • 刘游 - A fictional character in the story.
  • 东南山 - The eastern south mountain.
  • 蛇精 - Snake spirits.
  • 尖刀 - Sharp knives.
  • 火炮响器 - Firecrackers and noisemakers.

[612]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 埋畢 - Indicates the completion of a burial, suggesting a ritual or significant event.
  • 謂庄人曰 - Means 'told the villagers,' introducing a direct speech.
  • 于蛇将去时 - Refers to the moment when the snake is about to leave.
  • 急燃火炮 - Means 'quickly ignite firecrackers,' an action taken to scare the snake.
  • 共敲响器 - Means 'together strike noisy instruments,' another method to scare the snake.
  • 以惊之 - Means 'to frighten it,' indicating the purpose of the actions.
  • 众共应诺 - Means 'the crowd collectively agreed,' showing the villagers' compliance.
  • 蛇受惊，急去 - Means 'the snake was frightened and hurried away,' describing the snake's reaction.
  • 至埋刀处 - Means 'to the place where the knife was buried,' indicating a specific location.
  • 蛇身重 - Means 'the snake's body was heavy,' describing the snake's condition.
  • 草益偃 - Means 'the grass bent more,' indicating the snake's weight affecting the environment.
  • 刀尖大露 - Means 'the tip of the knife was prominently exposed,' describing the visibility of the knife.
  • 刺蛇腹 - Means 'pierced the snake's abdomen,' indicating an injury.
  • 蛇痛，行益急 - Means 'the snake was in pain and moved even faster,' describing the snake's reaction to the injury.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 惟笃勤俭，可免冻馁 - Means 'only by being diligent and thrifty can one avoid cold and hunger,' emphasizing the importance of hard work and frugality.
  • 使上无老，即与子饿死，命也，亦不怨天 - Means 'if there were no elderly person, I would starve with my child, it is fate, and I would not blame heaven,' expressing resignation and filial piety.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 氏 - Refers to a person or family, likely a commoner.
  • 乡人 - Refers to villagers or neighbors, indicating a rural setting.
  • 步蝗 - Refers to walking locusts, a type of locust that moves slowly.
  • 飞蝗 - Refers to flying locusts, a type of locust that can fly.
  • 翁 - Refers to an elderly man, likely the person's father.
  • 天灾 - Refers to natural disasters, emphasizing the uncontrollable nature of the event.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The remaining property was insufficient for food. Only by being diligent and thrifty could one avoid cold and hunger. The person ate simple food and personally cultivated and harvested. The villagers saw this and often deeply sighed.
  • When the locusts were about to move to the neighboring fields of the person, the person sat on the ground and cried bitterly, crying and saying, 'If there were no elderly person, I would starve with my child, it is fate, and I would not blame heaven.'
  • When the locusts had already infested the neighboring fields, the person cried even more bitterly, and the peasant woman tried to comfort him but he did not wake up. Later a woman said, 'You are not good at comforting him.' She then told the person, 'Your father is hungry and has called you for a long time.'
  • The person heard this, stopped crying immediately, wiped away tears, and returned home. The old man saw the person with red and swollen eyes, knowing it was because of the locusts, and told the person, 'This is a natural disaster, crying will not help. From now on, do not go to the fields, just accept it.'
  • The person followed the order, and the walking locusts jumped over the person's fields without causing any damage; the flying locusts also did not land on the person's fields. Six or seven people told me this story, but none of them knew the person's hometown or name.

  • 且鬻妾时，妾怀抱周岁幼子 - The presence of a young child added an emotional and moral dimension to the situation.

  • 子头破血出不止 - The severity of the injury highlights the brutality of the act.
  • 急哭数声即不哭 - The child's immediate death underscores the gravity of the situation.
  • 必已死去 - The certainty of the child's death adds to the emotional impact of the story.
  • 生怜之 - Feeling pity for someone in distress was a common human response.
  • 急令家人驾车送妇归 - Sending someone home quickly was a way to provide immediate assistance.
  • 桑氏曰 - 'Sang' is a common surname, indicating a person of some social standing.

  • 是事不可过夜，过夜则不免疑议 - The urgency of the situation and the potential for suspicion highlight the importance of prompt action.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 历年所寄束贽，除家中日用，应有赢余 - This sentence is elliptical, implying that the fees sent to the teacher should cover household expenses and leave some surplus.
  • 汝母来信，言家中苦寒异常 - This sentence is a bit ambiguous in terms of the exact nature of the hardship, but it suggests that the family is struggling financially.
  • 李公并不挽留，伪送之，而实引俊别墅居 - The sentence structure is somewhat complex, indicating that while the father appears to send the son away, he actually keeps him in a villa.
  • 李曲言于张，使从在塾读 - The sentence is elliptical, implying that the father pleads with the teacher to allow the son to continue studying.
  • 后公五子俱显达 - The sentence is elliptical, implying that all five sons of the father achieved prominence.
  • 俊亦得两榜，榜下以知县用 - The sentence is elliptical, implying that the son passed the imperial examinations and was appointed as a county magistrate.

  • 有言相劝 - Means 'has words to offer as advice,' indicating the ghost's intention to provide guidance.
  • 无瘾不必吸 - Means 'if not addicted, there is no need to smoke,' a straightforward piece of advice.
  • 有瘾不必忌 - Means 'if addicted, there is no need to abstain,' another paradoxical piece of advice.
  • 若云节食 - Means 'if one says to moderate,' referring to the idea of controlling one's intake.
  • 唯圣贤能之 - Means 'only the sage and the wise can do it,' emphasizing the difficulty of moderation.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鸦片烟 - Opium was a significant issue in China during the 19th century, leading to the Opium Wars and widespread social and economic problems.
  • 嗜食者 - The term '嗜食者' reflects the cultural perception of opium users as individuals who indulge in harmful habits.
  • 天良 - The concept of '天良' (conscience) is deeply rooted in Confucian ethics, emphasizing moral awareness and self-reflection.
  • 形消骨立 - This vivid description is a common literary device used to depict the physical toll of addiction.
  • 荡产破家 - The phrase highlights the severe financial consequences of opium addiction, which was a major social issue in late imperial China.
  • 覆宗绝祀 - This phrase underscores the cultural importance of family and lineage, suggesting that addiction can disrupt these fundamental social structures.
  • 断烟则上瘾 - This paradoxical statement reflects the complex nature of addiction, where the act of quitting can itself lead to a stronger desire for the substance.
  • 忌烟则丧生 - Another paradoxical statement, highlighting the perceived dangers of withdrawal, which was often exaggerated in popular discourse.
  • 为之奈何 - This phrase expresses a sense of helplessness, reflecting the difficulty of addressing the problem of addiction.
  • 烟鬼 - The term '烟鬼' is a colloquial and somewhat derogatory term for an opium addict, reflecting the negative social attitudes towards addiction.
  • 于此道阅历深矣 - The phrase '于此道' (in this path) refers to the long-term nature of opium addiction, emphasizing the depth of experience.
  • 有言相劝 - The ghost's offer of advice reflects the cultural belief in the power of supernatural beings to provide guidance.
  • 无瘾不必吸 - This advice is straightforward and practical, suggesting that non-addicts should avoid starting.
  • 有瘾不必忌 - This advice is more complex and paradoxical, reflecting the difficulty of breaking an addiction.
  • 若云节食 - The phrase '节食' (moderation) is used metaphorically to suggest controlling one's intake of opium.
  • 唯圣贤能之 - The reference to '圣贤' (sages and wise men) emphasizes the difficulty of moderation and the ideal of self-control.
  • 而圣贤又必不瘾于兹 - This rhetorical point suggests that true sages and wise men would not be addicted to opium, reinforcing the moral and ethical dimensions of the issue.

'總不宜與諸公接談' – 'it is altogether inappropriate for me to converse with the gentlemen.' Formal and archaic way of declining an invitation.

  • 二九一十八，二九不是一十八 - Numerical riddle. Literal meaning: 'two nines make eighteen, but two nines are not eighteen.' Play on numbers and words.
  • 內寓四字書一句 - '內寓' – 'contains within it.' '四字書一句' – 'a sentence from a four-character book' or 'a sentence from a classical four-character text.' Likely refers to a classical text composed in four-character phrases, such as the *Book of Rites* or the *Analects*.
  • 今九月九日，后日必有雨 - '后日' – 'the day after tomorrow.' '必有雨' – 'there will certainly be rain.'
  • 卿何好事好盛又为此也 - '卿' – term of address for a wife or close female companion. '何好事好盛' – 'why are you so fond of good things and grandeur?' or 'why are you so fond of doing good and seeking prominence?' '又为此也' – 'and doing this as well?'
  • 从此决不应召 - '从此' – 'from now on.' '决不应召' – 'will definitely not accept summonses anymore.'
  • 白始鸾续 - '白' – likely the name of the male character, possibly 'Bai' or 'White.' '鸾续' – term referring to a phoenix pair, often used metaphorically for a married couple or the resumption of a relationship. May also refer to the continuation of a marriage or the union of two people.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 陰教 - Teachings of the yin, referring to the feminine or spiritual teachings, possibly in contrast to the more active or yang principles. May also refer to Daoist or Confucian teachings associated with the feminine.
  • 九九算数 - Nine-Nine Calculation, a reference to the multiplication table in ancient Chinese mathematics. The riddle uses this as a basis for its wordplay.
  • 四字書 - Four-character books, referring to classical Chinese texts composed in four-character phrases, such as the *Analects of Confucius*, the *Book of Rites*, or the *Book of Changes*. These were common in ancient didactic literature.
  • 九月九日 - The ninth day of the ninth lunar month, traditionally the Double Ninth Festival, a time for climbing mountains and honoring ancestors. In this context, it is used as a date for the riddle.
  • 鸾续 - '鸾' is a mythical bird, often paired with '凤' (phoenix), symbolizing a harmonious or noble couple. '鸾续' may indicate the resumption or continuation of a relationship or marriage, suggesting the woman is returning to her husband or resuming her role in the household.
  • 白 - May be a personal name, possibly 'Bai' or 'White,' which is a common surname in Chinese history. The character '白' can also mean 'white' or 'clear,' and in some contexts, it can be a poetic or symbolic name.

  • 何待人言？ - Rhetorical question meaning 'what need is there for others to say it?' emphasizing self-evident duty.
  • 至言之不听，其人已不足齿数矣 - Judgmental phrase meaning 'if one refuses to heed sound advice, such a person is already unworthy of mention,' criticizing the stubborn individual.
  • 究之不能不养，不敢不继 - Concessive phrase meaning 'in the end, one cannot avoid nurturing and cannot dare not to continue the family line,' highlighting unavoidable moral responsibility.
  • 为不养不继者之榜样 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to set an example for those who do not nurture or continue the family line,' contrasting positive and negative behavior.
  • 而仍为天下之罪人也 - Final judgment meaning 'yet remains a sinner to the world,' condemning the person despite their actions.
  • 鬼狐遗方 - Classical phrase meaning 'formulas left behind by ghosts and foxes,' introducing supernatural remedies.
  • 吞盐卤者，血凝即死 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'those who swallow salt brine will have their blood congeal and die,' describing a medical condition.
  • 以未点卤之豆腐汁灌之即愈 - Remedy phrase meaning 'if they are given undiluted soy milk, they will recover,' describing a treatment.
  • 其理易明也 - Concluding phrase meaning 'the reasoning is easy to understand,' explaining the logic behind the remedy.
  • 如比近无卖豆腐家，急取豆研汁，亦可济事 - Conditional phrase meaning 'if there is no nearby tofu shop, quickly grind soybeans into juice, and it can still help,' providing an alternative solution.
  • 吞信石者，急用防风一两，研末，清水调灌，亦愈 - Remedy phrase meaning 'for those who swallow arsenic, quickly use one liang of Fangfeng, grind it into powder, mix with water, and administer it, and they will recover,' describing another treatment.
  • 防风只去风湿之药，而能化砒毒 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'Fangfeng is only a wind-dampness remedy, yet it can neutralize arsenic poison,' highlighting the unexpected efficacy.
  • 令人不解 - Concluding phrase meaning 'which is puzzling,' expressing the author's bewilderment.
  • 按《本草》，防风能杀附子毒 - Reference meaning 'according to the *Bencao* (Herbal), Fangfeng can neutralize the toxicity of Fuzi (aconite),' citing a classical medical text.
  • 夫附子极热，信石亦极热 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'Fuzi is extremely hot, and arsenic is also extremely hot,' explaining the nature of the substances.
  • 防风能解信石热毒，或即以其有杀附子热毒之能乎？ - Speculative phrase meaning 'Could it be that Fangfeng's ability to neutralize the heat toxicity of arsenic is due to its ability to neutralize the heat toxicity of Fuzi?' proposing a possible explanation.
  • 二方前辈著述多载之 - Reference meaning 'many predecessors' writings have recorded these two formulas,' acknowledging earlier sources.
  • 余恐传闻不广，故复录于此 - Concluding phrase meaning 'I am concerned that these remedies are not widely known, so I am recording them again here,' explaining the author's motivation for repeating the information.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 同治三年歲次甲子秋八月 - Traditional Chinese dating format. '同治三年' refers to the third year of the Tongzhi era (1864). '歲次甲子' is the sexagenary cycle year corresponding to 'Jia Zi' (the first year of the 60-year cycle), which aligns with 1864. '秋八月' means 'the eighth month of autumn.'
  • 世晚王廷槐植三拜讀 - A formal expression of reverence and submission. '世晚' may be a humble self-reference, possibly meaning 'a later generation' or 'a descendant.' '王廷槐植' is likely the name of the person (possibly a relative or student) who is reading the text. '三拜讀' means 'to read it with three bows' or 'to read it with deep reverence,' indicating a ritual of respect.
  • 昔昌黎氏曰 - A reference to 'Changli' (昌黎), the pen name of Han Yu (韩愈), a famous Tang dynasty literary figure and reformer. This is a common way to cite classical authors in Chinese.
  • 物不得其平則鳴 - A well-known saying from Han Yu. It means 'When things are not in balance, they cry out' or 'When things are not level, they make a sound.' It is a metaphor for the human urge to express injustice or dissatisfaction.
  • 有能外于此者乎 - A rhetorical question meaning 'Is there anyone who can be outside of this?' or 'Is there anyone who can escape this?'
  • 挹精华于典籍，抒愤懑于胸臆 - 'To draw the essence from the classics, and to vent one’s anger and sorrow from the heart.' This is a poetic expression of literary creation as both intellectual and emotional.
  • 礑扌离词力托和平 - This phrase is challenging due to the unusual characters '礑' and '扌离.' '礑' may be a misspelling or variant of '礁' (reef) or '濤' (wave), but in the context of literary expression, it may mean 'to shake' or 'to stir.' '扌离' appears to be a misspelled or variant form of '离' (to separate) or '摛' (to spread out, as in spreading out words). The phrase may mean 'the power of words shakes or separates from peace' – a metaphor for the emotional force of language.
  • 假之以鳴 - 'To use it to cry out' or 'to allow it to express itself.' This is a metaphor for giving voice to one's inner turmoil or injustice.
  • 有不能已于中者 - 'There is something that cannot be held back from within,' meaning an inner compulsion or feeling that must be expressed.
  • 稗官 - Refers to 'bi guan' (wild officials), a term for unofficial or semi-official historical records, often in the form of novels or stories.
  • 上溯《齊諧》，下迨《聊齋》 - 'From the top to *Qi Heng* (a classical collection of strange tales), and down to *Liaozhai* (a famous Qing dynasty collection of supernatural stories).' This shows a lineage of strange tales and fiction.
  • 吾堂伯母解氏侄子鏡者 - A complex phrase. '吾堂伯母' is 'my aunt on my father’s side’s elder sister' or 'my aunt from the same clan.' '解氏' is the family name, '侄子' is 'nephew,' and '鏡' is the name. So the phrase refers to 'my cousin (nephew) Jing, son of my aunt from the Jie family.'
  • 有高世之才，無偶世之遇 - 'He has talent surpassing the world, but no opportunity equal to the world.' A contrast between talent and lack of fortune.
  • 年逾知命，而竟一芹莫采 - 'Beyond the age of knowing one's fate (fifty), yet no one has picked even a single sprig of qin.' '一芹' is a metaphor for a small offering or opportunity, '莫采' means 'no one has picked it.' The phrase conveys that despite his age and talent, the person has received no recognition.
  • 彼倉之位置，將欲假之以鳴乎 - 'Is that position (of the granary) meant to allow him to cry out?' A metaphor suggesting that the person is in a position of neglect, but perhaps meant to express something.
  • 力扶名教 - 'To strongly support the teachings of Confucianism or the moral order.'
  • 資人考鏡、啟人聰明 - 'To provide people with the means to examine and reflect, and to enlighten their wisdom.'
  • 天下智者見之，謂之智 - 'When the wise of the world see it, they call it wise.' This is a self-referential statement affirming the value of the work.
  • 殆即是录之定评欤 - 'It is perhaps the definitive evaluation of this collection.' A closing remark affirming the work’s merit.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 同治三年 - The third year of the Tongzhi era (1864), during the late Qing dynasty.

  • 文如庄列与骚萧 - Comparison to the styles of Zhuangzi (庄子), Liezi (列子), and the *Li Sao* (离骚) by Qu Yuan, suggesting a blend of philosophical, fantastical, and poetic elements.

  • 笔引虹 - Metaphorical description of the text's flow, where '笔引虹' (the brush leads like a rainbow) suggests a vibrant and colorful style.
  • 前身应是聊斋翁 - Reference to the author of *Liaozhai Zhiyi* (Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio), suggesting that the writer has a similar style or talent.

  • 著手成春 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'to create spring with a touch,' suggesting that the writer has a magical or transformative power.

  • 癸丑冬，友人案头有《益智录》数篇，披读之下，不禁欣赏 - Temporal marker (癸丑冬, Winter of 1893) and description of reading and enjoying a few chapters of *Yizhi Lu*.
  • 乙卯春，先生以全集相寄，并命予为序 - Temporal marker (乙卯春, Spring of 1895) and description of receiving the complete work and being asked to write a preface.

  • 蘼芜日日生，豆蔻年年老 - Metaphorical expression using 'millefolium' and 'clove-bud' to symbolize the passage of time and the aging of youth.
  • 作鬼的 - Refers to ghosts or spirits, often used in a colloquial or literary sense.
  • 相思何日了 - Common expression meaning 'when will the longing end,' often used in romantic or nostalgic contexts.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 长吉 - Refers to Li He (李贺), a famous poet of the Tang Dynasty known for his unique and often melancholic style.
  • 董狐 - Refers to Dong Fu, a historian from the Spring and Autumn period known for his impartial and truthful records.
  • 班马 - Refers to Ban Gu and Sima Qian, two famous historians and writers from the Han Dynasty.
  • 聊斋志异 - Famous collection of short stories by Pu Songling, often considered a masterpiece of Chinese literature.
  • 池北偶谈 - Collection of anecdotes and stories by Wang Shizhen.
  • 虞初新志 - Collection of stories and essays by various authors, often considered a precursor to modern short story collections.

  • 寄托遥深见性情 - Implies that deep emotions and character are revealed through writing.
  • 百琲珍珠穿穴密 - Metaphor for the intricate and valuable nature of the writing, like pearls strung together.

  • 如来妙演莲华法 - Refers to the Buddha's profound teachings, often symbolized by the lotus flower.
  • 普作慈航渡众生 - Indicates the universal compassion and salvation offered by the Buddha.

  • 眼际烟云观宇宙 - Metaphor for viewing the universe as if through a misty lens, suggesting a broad and philosophical perspective.
